perturbation.original_text: The receiving party agrees that it will at all times maintain the confidentiality of any Confidential Information communicated to it by or on behalf of the disclosing party; provided that in the event the receiving party becomes legally compelled (by deposition, interrogatory, requests for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Page 1 ​ of 4 Information, or the receiving party determines that it is obligated by statute or governmental regulation to disclose any of the Confidential Information, the receiving party shall provide the disclosing party with prompt NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT written notice of such requirement so that the disclosing party, if possible, may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party agrees to maintain the confidentiality of Confidential Information communicated to it by the disclosing party; provided that if the receiving party believes it is required to disclose Confidential Information due to legal processes, it should endeavor to notify the disclosing party, potentially allowing them to seek a protective order or other remedy.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the receiving party 'shall provide prompt written notice' when legally compelled to disclose information. The modified text uses weaker language ('should endeavor to notify') and introduces uncertainty about whether the disclosing party will actually seek a protective order. This weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality and provide notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act protects trade secrets. By weakening the notification requirement when disclosure is compelled, the modified text makes it more difficult for the disclosing party to protect its trade secrets, potentially leading to a violation of the Act if the confidential information constitutes a trade secret and is misappropriated due to the lack of timely notification and opportunity to seek legal protection.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates that the receiving party "shall provide prompt written notice" if legally compelled to disclose confidential information. The modified text weakens this obligation by stating the receiving party "should endeavor to notify," making the notification discretionary. This undermines the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating the Economic Espionage Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 18 USC 1832, 'Theft of trade secrets,' which indicates the legal importance of protecting such information. By weakening the notification requirement, the modified text directly contradicts the aim of protecting trade secrets, as the disclosing party may not have the opportunity to seek a protective order, potentially leading to misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall not directly or indirectly contact any shareholders, officers, employees, affiliates, agents, lending institutions, customers or suppliers of the disclosing party in connection with a possible transaction involving the disclosing party without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party should generally avoid contacting shareholders, officers, employees, affiliates, agents, lending institutions, customers or suppliers of the disclosing party in connection with a possible transaction involving the disclosing party without seeking consent from the disclosing party, and the disclosing party will try to be reasonable when considering such requests.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation ('shall not contact...without prior written consent, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld'). The modified text weakens this by using 'should generally avoid' and 'seeking consent'. The phrase 'try to be reasonable' is vague and subjective, potentially allowing the disclosing party to arbitrarily withhold consent. This makes the receiving party's obligation much less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text ensures controlled information flow. By making the restrictions vaguer, the amended text increases the risk of market manipulation. The disclosing party could arbitrarily withhold consent for contact, hindering due diligence and potentially violating the Securities Exchange Act if this withholding results in unfair or misleading market practices concerning a potential securities transaction.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 78i - Manipulation of security prices | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 78i - Manipulation of security prices U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Transactions relating to purchase or sale of security It shall be unlawful for any person , directly or indirectly, by the use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce , or of any facility of any national securities exchange, or for any member of a national securities exchange— (1) For the purpose of creating a false or misleading appearance of active trading in any security other than a government security , or a false or misleading appearance with respect to the market for any such security , (A) to effect any transaction in such security which involves no change in the beneficial ownership thereof, or (B) to enter an order or orders for the purchase of such security with the knowledge that an order or orders of substantially the same size, at substantially the same time, and at substantially the same price, for the sale of any such security, has been or will be entered by or for the same or different parties, or (C) to enter any order or orders for the sale of any such security with the knowledge that an order or orders of substantially the same size, at substantially the same time, and at substantially the same price, for the purchase of such security, has been or will be entered by or for the same or different parties. (2) To effect, alone or with 1 or more other persons , a series of transactions in any security registered on a national securities exchange , any security not so registered, or in connection with any security-based swap or security-based swap agreement with respect to such security creating actual or apparent active trading in such security, or raising or depressing the price of such security, for the purpose of inducing
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, weakening the original obligation regarding contacting shareholders. The original text stated 'shall not...without prior written consent, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld,' creating a clear directive. The changed text uses 'should generally avoid' and 'seeking consent,' making the obligation less enforceable. The Law Explanation suggests this could lead to market manipulation and violate the Securities Exchange Act. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2, both citing 15 USC 78i, address the 'Manipulation of security prices' and deem it unlawful for any person to create 'a false or misleading appearance of active trading'. While the snippets don't directly address the permissibility of contact, hindering due diligence by arbitrarily withholding consent, as the Law Explanation suggests, could potentially contribute to unfair market practices related to securities transactions, thereby contradicting the intent of 15 USC 78i.

perturbation.original_text: All right, title and interest in and to the Confidential Information shall remain the exclusive property of the disclosing party and the Confidential Information shall be held in trust and confidence by the receiving party for the disclosing party. No interest, license or any right respecting the Confidential Information, other than set out herein, is granted to the receiving party hereunder by implication or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: All right, title and interest in and to the Confidential Information should generally remain with the disclosing party, and the receiving party will endeavor to treat the Confidential Information responsibly. No explicit rights are granted to the receiving party unless specifically stated.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that all rights remain the exclusive property of the disclosing party and the receiving party holds the information 'in trust and confidence'. The modified text weakens this by using 'should generally remain' and 'endeavor to treat responsibly'. This creates ambiguity about the receiving party's obligations and dilutes the ownership rights of the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. By weakening the obligation to hold Confidential Information in trust and confidence, the modified text makes it harder to prove misappropriation under the DTSA, because it creates uncertainty about the receiving party's duty to protect the information. The phrase 'endeavor to treat the confidential information responsibly' is not definitive enough to align with the legal standards for trade secret protection.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation concerning confidential information, creating a contradiction. While the original text stipulates that 'all right, title and interest' remain with the disclosing party and that the receiving party holds the information 'in trust and confidence,' the changed text uses softer language like 'should generally remain' and 'endeavor to treat responsibly.' This introduces ambiguity regarding the receiving party's duty, making it harder to prove misappropriation. The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as explained by the LLM, provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation, but proving this becomes more difficult when the obligation to protect the information is weakened. The law snippet provided (18 USC 1836) refers to 'Civil proceedings' related to violations. The original text clearly establishes ownership and responsibility, whereas the changed text makes these aspects ambiguous and harder to enforce, which would contradict the intent of DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the disclosing party and its shareholders, directors, officers, employees, representatives and professional advisors from any damage, loss, cost or liability (including reasonable legal fees and the cost of enforcing this Agreement) owed to a third party arising out of the breach by the receiving party of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party may consider providing some level of indemnification to the disclosing party and its related parties for certain damages, losses, costs, or liabilities resulting from a breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear obligation for the receiving party to indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the disclosing party. The modified text makes this obligation discretionary and vague by using 'may consider providing some level of indemnification' and 'certain damages'. The deletion of 'defend' and 'hold harmless' further weakens the protection afforded to the disclosing party. This introduces ambiguity about the scope and extent of the indemnification.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the confidential information is related to a patentable invention, the weakened indemnification clause may contradict the intent of the Patent Act. By limiting the indemnification obligation, the disclosing party may bear significant costs in defending against third-party claims arising from the receiving party's misuse of confidential information related to the invention. This would undermine the patent holder's ability to enforce their rights and recover damages as intended by 35 U.S. Code § 284.

When the damages are not found by a jury, the court shall assess them. In either event the court may increase the damages up to three times the amount
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 284 - Damages | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 284 - Damages U.S. Code Notes prev | next Upon finding for the claimant the court shall award the claimant damages adequate to compensate for the infringement, but in no event less than a reasonable royalty for the use made of the invention by the infringer, together with interest and costs as fixed by the court. When the damages are not found by a jury, the court shall assess them. In either event the court may increase the damages up to three times the amount found or assessed. Increased damages under this paragraph shall not apply to provisional rights under section 154(d). The court may receive expert testimony as an aid to the determination of damages or of what royalty would be reasonable under the circumstances. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 813 ; Pub. L. 106–113, div. B, § 1000(a)(9) [title IV, § 4507(9)] , Nov. 29, 1999 , 113 Stat. 1536 , 1501A–566; Pub. L. 112–29, § 20(j) , Sept. 16, 2011 , 125 Stat. 335 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on Title 35, U.S.C., 1946 ed., §§ 67 and 70, part (R.S. 4919; R.S. 4921, amended (1) Mar. 3, 1897, ch. 391, § 6, 29 Stat. 694 , (2) Feb. 18, 1922, ch. 58, § 8, 42 Stat. 392 , (3) Aug. 1, 1946, ch. 726, § 1, 60 Stat. 778 ). This section consolidates the provisions relating to damages in R.S. 4919 and 4921, with some changes in language. Editorial Notes Amendments 2011—Second par. Pub. L. 112–29 struck out “of this title” after “154(d)”. 1999—Second par. Pub. L. 106–113 inserted at end “Increased damages under this paragraph shall not apply to provisional rights under section 154(d) of this title .” Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2011 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 112–29 effective upon the expiration of the 1-year period beginning on Sept. 16, 2011 , and applicable to proceedings commenced on or after that effective
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the indemnification obligation of the receiving party, potentially contradicting the intent of 35 U.S. Code § 284, which aims to ensure adequate compensation for patent infringement. The original text's mandatory language ('will indemnify, defend and hold harmless') is replaced with discretionary language ('may consider providing some level of indemnification'), introducing ambiguity about the scope and extent of the receiving party's liability. This weakened protection could result in the disclosing party bearing significant costs related to third-party claims, undermining their ability to recover damages for infringement as intended by 35 U.S.C. § 284, which states that 'the court shall award the claimant damages adequate to compensate for the infringement'. The modified text creates an ambiguity and thus contradicts the legal protection.
